skill: Compute the determinant of a 3x3 matrix using cofactor expansion

messages.content:
# Problem

Compute the determinant of the following matrix using **cofactor expansion**. Choose your expansion row or column strategically.

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 0 \\ 5 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Show your work, including which row or column you expanded along and why.

# Let me show you how

**Strategy first:** With cofactor expansion, you pick the row or column with the **most zeros** — each zero kills a term, saving work. Here, **column 2** has two zeros (the 0's in rows 1 and 3). That's our target.

## The formula

Expanding along column $j$:
$$\det(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} (-1)^{i+j} \, a_{ij} \, M_{ij}$$

where $M_{ij}$ is the **minor** — the determinant of the 2×2 matrix left after deleting row $i$ and column $j$.

## Expanding along column 2

Entries in column 2: $a_{12}=0$, $a_{22}=4$, $a_{32}=0$.

Only the middle term survives:

$$\det(A) = (-1)^{2+2} \cdot 4 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

The sign $(-1)^{2+2} = +1$. The 2×2 determinant is $(2)(6) - (3)(5) = 12 - 15 = -3$.

$$\det(A) = 1 \cdot 4 \cdot (-3) = -12$$

## The sign pattern (memorize this)

$$\begin{bmatrix} + & - & + \\ - & + & - \\ + & - & + \end{bmatrix}$$

The position $(i,j)$ gets sign $(-1)^{i+j}$.

---

**Your turn — same idea, new matrix:**

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Which row or column would you expand along, and why? (Don't compute yet — just pick and justify.)
